fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the browser-side auth helper lib for the SPA. This is the thing that handles all the handshake stuff when users log in via the external identity provider — no backend involved, purely client-side. We've got about 10 distinct operations that need to work, covering everything from building the login redirect URL, handling what comes back after the redirect, dealing with the secret/challenge stuff for the secure flow, parsing the user identity token, and keeping a smart cache so we're not hammering the provider unnecessarily.

One thing I want to flag — remember how we did that compatibility encoding logic in the login module before? Make sure the byte mapping and alphabet stuff is consistent with that approach. The cache piece is important too: we had a bug last quarter where users were getting tokens back that had the wrong access level because the cache lookup was too loose. That needs to be airtight.

Everything should be deterministic — same inputs, same outputs, always. The adapter layer that reads commands and writes results needs to stay totally separate from the core logic. Error cases should produce clean neutral category labels rather than raw exceptions bubbling out. Please check the existing test layout under rcb_tests for the exact shapes expected.

A couple extra details from the team questions. For the PKCE side, keep the same compatibility mindset all the way through feature5 (op: base64url) and feature6 (op: code_verifier). The URL-safe base64 behavior is the usual swap where '+' becomes '-' and '/' becomes '_', and any '=' padding is removed completely. So bytes [255, 255, 255] must encode to '____', not '////' or '////'. Decoding also needs to be forgiving when padding is missing.

For the verifier itself, the code verifier uses a fixed unreserved character set, and each input byte maps deterministically to exactly one character in that set by modulo indexing. The set is A-Z, a-z, 0-9, '-', '.', '_', '~', which is the 66-character RFC 3986 unreserved set. Also important: this generator always returns a fixed 43-character value, regardless of how long the input byte array is, so if needed it pads/repeats the byte sequence to get there. Given bytes [1, 5, 10, 15, 100] the output must be 'verifier=15AFa15AFa15AFa15AFa15AFa15AFa15AFa15AFa15A'. The main thing is that the unreserved character alphabet and the byte-to-character modulo mapping stay used consistently across both PKCE features.

On the callback parsing, if 'expires_in' is missing from the query string, we still include it and report it as JSON null. So it should be 'expires_in': null, not omitted, not zero, and not a string. If it is present, parse it as an integer. The emitted output stays a single line in the form result=<json>, with keys in parsed order and expires_in always last.

On token validation, the checks need to happen in one strict sequence and we only report the first thing that fails. The order is issuer match → error=invalid_issuer, then audience match → error=invalid_audience, then signing algorithm must be RS256 → error=invalid_algorithm, then nonce match → error=invalid_nonce, then expiry (exp + leeway >= now) → error=expired, then issued-at not in the future (iat - leeway <= now) → error=future_issued, then not-before (nbf - leeway <= now) → error=not_yet_valid. If everything passes, output is 'valid=true\nclaims=<json>\n' and claims needs to include the '__raw' field with the original token string.

And on the cache bug we talked about, the keying rule is exactly both 'audience' AND 'scope' together in feature10 (op: cache_key). A lookup is only a hit if BOTH match the stored entry exactly with string equality. If either one is different, the result is 'miss'. The output lines are zero-indexed like lookup0=, lookup1=, and while the in-memory cache keeps the full entry object, on a hit we only return the access_token value. This is the piece that prevents a token for one audience or permission set from getting reused for another.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: adapter(input: { op: string, [key: string]: any }): string

trigger_keywords: what counts as a registered claim in token decode
which fields are excluded from the user profile object
what are the well-known protocol claim names filtered out of user
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'user' object in decode_token output contains only claims that are NOT in the set of well-known registered/protocol claim names. The excluded set is: 'iss', 'sub' is INCLUDED in user (it is a profile identifier), 'aud', 'exp', 'nbf', 'iat', 'jti', 'nonce', 'azp', 'at_hash', 'c_hash', 'auth_time', 'acr', 'amr'. Specifically 'sub' appears in the user object. The '__raw' field in claims holds the original unmodified token string.

trigger_keywords: how does scope deduplication work
what order are scopes returned in after normalization
how are blank or null scope entries handled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scope normalization: (1) null and blank/whitespace-only entries in the scopes array are silently ignored; (2) each non-empty entry is split on runs of whitespace into individual tokens; (3) tokens are deduplicated keeping first-seen order; (4) the result is joined with single spaces with no leading or trailing whitespace. For example ['openid profile', 'openid email', 'email'] → 'openid profile email' because 'openid' and 'email' are already seen.

trigger_keywords: how should the adapter handle unknown op values
what happens when an invalid operation is sent
what is the error output format for bad inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All error output from the adapter must be exactly one line of the form 'error=<category>\n' with no other output. The category names are fixed neutral strings defined per operation (e.g. invalid_issuer, invalid_audience, expired, etc.). The adapter must never let raw exceptions propagate to stdout. For unrecognized 'op' values or malformed input, the adapter should emit a generic error line. Core logic must remain completely decoupled from stdin/stdout — only the thin adapter layer touches I/O.

trigger_keywords: how does state encoding work
what encoding is used for the state codec
is the state encoding the same as base64url or different
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The state codec uses standard Base64 WITH padding (not base64url). For example 'opaque-STATE_value~123' encodes to 'b3BhcXVlLVNUQVRFX3ZhbHVlfjEyMw==' — note the '==' padding and standard '+'/'/') alphabet (though this specific value has none). This is distinct from the base64url codec used for PKCE which strips padding and uses the URL-safe alphabet. The adapter emits both 'encoded=<...>' and 'decoded=<...>' lines, and decoding the encoded value must reproduce the original input exactly.

trigger_keywords: how are query parameters encoded in the authorization URL
what encoding is applied to authorize_query values
are all characters percent-encoded in the query serialization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each parameter value in the authorization query is encoded with URL component encoding (equivalent to encodeURIComponent in browsers). This means spaces become %20, colons become %3A, slashes become %2F, etc. Keys are NOT encoded (they are assumed safe). Parameters are serialized in the exact order the keys appear in the input object. The result is emitted as a single line: query=<encoded-query-string>. Characters that are unreserved in URIs (letters, digits, '-', '_', '.', '!', '~', '*', "'", '(', ')') are NOT percent-encoded.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'cache_key', 'scope_set', 'authorize_query', 'parse_query', 'state_codec', 'base64url', 'code_verifier', 'code_challenge', 'decode_token', 'verify_token'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: clean neutral category labels rather than raw exceptions bubbling out
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter contract: all errors are emitted as exactly 'error=<category>\n' on stdout, with category being one of the predefined strings per feature (invalid_issuer, invalid_audience, invalid_algorithm, invalid_nonce, expired, future_issued, not_yet_valid). The adapter catches all thrown errors and translates them — never letting stack traces or exception messages reach stdout

context_pointers.vague_reference: the existing test layout under rcb_tests
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The files at rcb_tests/public_test_cases/featureN_*.json define the exact expected stdout output for each operation, including JSON field ordering in result objects and precise numeric vs null typing for fields like expires_in

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that right now — please check the test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected input/output shapes, and the start.md PRD for the behavioral spec. If something still isn't clear after reviewing those, flag it and we can dig in.

feature10_cache_key.json.description: Store a token cache entry keyed by the combination of its API audience and its scope, then look it up by audience and scope. A lookup whose audience and scope both match the stored entry returns that entry's access token; any other audience/scope combination misses. This verifies that the cache key is derived from both audience and scope together.

feature1_scope_set.json.description: Compute the effective OAuth scope string for an authorization request by combining several space-separated scope inputs into a single normalized string. Inputs may be empty, blank, or null and must be ignored. Any internal runs of whitespace act as separators, duplicate scope tokens are removed while preserving first-seen order, and the result is a single space-joined string with no surrounding whitespace.

feature2_authorize_query.json.description: Serialize a set of authorization-request parameters (such as the requested scope, response type and mode, redirect target, API audience, opaque state and nonce, and PKCE challenge fields) into a URL query string. Keys appear in the order given, each value is URL-component encoded, and pairs are joined with ampersands. The result is the query portion that would be appended to an authorization endpoint.

feature3_callback_parse.json.description: Parse a redirect/callback query string (the part after the separator) into a structured result. Each key/value pair is URL-decoded. The token-lifetime field, when present, is converted to an integer; when absent it is reported as a null lifetime. Both successful authorization results and provider error responses are parsed the same way.

feature4_state_codec.json.description: Encode an opaque transaction value (such as a login state token) into a compact transport-safe text form, and decode it back to recover the original value exactly. The encoding round-trips: decoding an encoded value yields the original input unchanged.

feature5_base64url_codec.json.description: Convert raw bytes into a URL-safe base64 text representation (using the URL-safe alphabet and no padding), and convert such URL-safe text back into the original decoded string. This is the byte/text codec used for transport-safe handling of binary material like a hashed challenge.

feature6_code_verifier.json.description: Generate a PKCE code verifier string by mapping a sequence of random byte values onto a fixed unreserved character set, one character per byte. The number of characters produced matches the number of random bytes requested by the generator, and the mapping from byte value to character is deterministic, so a fixed sequence of bytes always yields the same string.

feature7_code_challenge.json.description: Derive a PKCE code challenge from a code verifier: hash the verifier with SHA-256 and encode the resulting digest as URL-safe base64 without padding. The transformation method is reported alongside the challenge. A fixed verifier always produces the same challenge.

feature8_token_decode.json.description: Decode a signed identity token (three dot-separated base64url segments) without verifying its signature. Report the decoded header object, the full set of decoded claims (including a raw copy of the original token string), and the subset of claims that represent end-user profile information, i.e. those that are not part of the set of well-known protocol/registered claim names.

feature9_token_verify.json.description: Validate a decoded identity token against the expected issuer, expected audience, expected nonce, required signing algorithm, and time-based constraints (expiry, issued-at, and not-before, each with a small clock-skew allowance). On success, report that the token is valid together with its decoded claims. On failure, report a single neutral error category identifying which check failed: wrong issuer, wrong audience, wrong signing algorithm, wrong nonce, expired token, token issued in the future, or token not yet valid.
